coredata.dc:title: Iron ion sensing and in vitro and in vivo imaging based on bright blue-fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Dan
Zhao, Zhonghua
Niu, Wenjing
Shi, Lihong
Yang, Yongxing
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we propose an eco-friendly synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and ingeniously design a rapid and label-free “turn-off” sensing platform for ultrasensitive recognition of Fe3+ in vitro and in vivo. CDs with extraordinary advantages involving exceptional stability, ultra-low toxicity as well as admirable biocompatibility were simply prepared via one-step hydrothermal strategy of Caulis polygoni multiflora. Result indicated that as-acquired CDs not only exhibit excitation dependency, but also have a high quantum yield of (QY) up to 42%. Miraculously, the fluorescence of CDs can be extinguished sharply by Fe3+ because of static quenching effect with linear range of 0–400 µM, yielding a detection limit of 0.025 μM. Benefiting from these characteristics, CDs have been extended for multicolourful imaging and tracking Fe3+ fluctuations in living cells. Bioimaging of zebrafish larvae exposed to CDs confirmed that it is smoothly circulated to other tissues and organs owing to their small size. Eventually, as-prepared CDs have been implemented for the real-time detection of Fe3+ in nude mice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blue fluorescence
Carbon dots
Iron ion
Imaging
Sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of CDs.
(A) TEM and (B) AFM images of CDs.
(A) XPS spectra of CDs. (B–D) C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of CDs, respectively. (E) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
(A) UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (B) Excitation–emission spectra of CDs. (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence responses of CDs toward various ions (400 μM). (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs under different amounts of Fe3+. (C) Linear relationship between Ln(F/F0 + 0.11) and Fe3+ concentrations.

(A) UV–vis spectrum of Fe3+ and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (B) Fluorescence decay of CDs and CDs/Fe3+. (C) Absorption of CDs with various concentrations of Fe3+. (D) Zeta potentials of CDs and CDs/Fe3+.

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of HepG-2 cells incubated with CDs. (Blue channel: λex/λem 405 nm/435–450 nm) (B) Confocal fluorescence images of HepG-2 cells incubated with CDs. (Green channel: λex/λem 488 nm/492–577 nm) (C) Confocal fluorescence images of HepG-2 cells incubated with CDs. (Orange channel: λex/λem 514 nm/580–620 nm) (D) Overlay images of (A) and (B). (E) Overlay images of (A) and (C). (F) Overlay images of (B) and (C).
Confocal fluorescence images of HepG-2 cells stained CDs after the addition of Fe3+.
Confocal images of zebrafish larvae treated with different amounts of CDs.
Fluorescence images of nude mice via tail intravenous injection of CDs at 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9 h.
Fluorescence images of nude mice with CDs/Fe3+ with extended time.

head.ce:title: Iron ion sensing and in vitro and in vivo imaging based on bright blue-fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we propose an eco-friendly synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and ingeniously design a rapid and label-free “turn-off” sensing platform for ultrasensitive recognition of Fe3+ in vitro and in vivo. CDs with extraordinary advantages involving exceptional stability, ultra-low toxicity as well as admirable biocompatibility were simply prepared via one-step hydrothermal strategy of Caulis polygoni multiflora. Result indicated that as-acquired CDs not only exhibit excitation dependency, but also have a high quantum yield of (QY) up to 42%. Miraculously, the fluorescence of CDs can be extinguished sharply by Fe3+ because of static quenching effect with linear range of 0–400 µM, yielding a detection limit of 0.025 μM. Benefiting from these characteristics, CDs have been extended for multicolourful imaging and tracking Fe3+ fluctuations in living cells. Bioimaging of zebrafish larvae exposed to CDs confirmed that it is smoothly circulated to other tissues and organs owing to their small size. Eventually, as-prepared CDs have been implemented for the real-time detection of Fe3+ in nude mice.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe) is not only one of the most abundant elements on earth, but also the utmost indispensable elements in human body, which acts as the fundamental structure of hemoglobin and participates in multiple pathological and physiological processes such as exchange at the cellular level, nucleic acid synthesis, catalysis, oxygen transportation, and so forth [1–3]. As a heavy metal, iron is extensively used in the industry, which causes water pollution and has raised a great deal of concern over environmental hazard. In addition, deficiency or overloading of Fe3+ can trigger undesirable influences on immunity, and thus induce various kinds of serious diseases including anemia, heart attack, kidney damages and intellectual deterioration [4,5]. For these reasons, the precise and reliable determination of Fe3+ levels becomes of great significance from the viewpoint of human health or environmental pollution.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as the emerging nanomaterials, have aroused an increasing amount of scientific attention due to significant advantages including exceptional water solubility, charming optical features, fabulous photostability and outstanding biocompatibility. Taking advantage of these merits, CDs have presented enormous potentials for diverse applications in bioimaging [6,7], drug delivery [8–10], optoelectronic devices [11–13] and chemical sensing [14–16]. Up to date, numerous attempts have been employed favorably for the synthesis of CDs which typically consist of either top-down or bottom-up routes. As is known to all, top-down approaches usually have arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis, and so forth [17–19], which suffer from various inherent drawbacks including expensive devices, severe synthetic conditions and sophisticated preparation procedures, resulting in manufacturing difficulties and high costs for mass production. Besides, bottom-up strategies mainly involve several types such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal treatment as well as microwave-assisted synthesis [20–22]. Notably, tremendous attention has been focused on hydrothermal method on account of its benefits such as simple operation, low energy-consumption and easy control. Therefore, it is widely accepted that fluorescent CDs were synthetized by hydrothermal route and a significant number of works have been frequently reported in recent years.

So far, plenty of advanced fluorescent nanomaterials have been essentially utilized for quantitative analysis of Fe3+, such as luminescent metal − organic frameworks, coordination polymers, organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, and so forth [23–26], which possess various limitations including narrow excitation, higher toxic, low fluorescence intensity and poor photo‐stability. A very significant aspect of fluorescent CDs that has aroused great excitement is their cost-effective preparation, which cannot be achieved for the above conventional fluorescent nanomaterials. Based on these considerations, it is desirable to search for benign materials that hold distinguished optical properties and low costs for large-scale synthesis. It is worth noting that CDs-based florescent Fe3+ probes as the excellent candidate have been developed rapidly and recognized as a hallmark analytical platform for Fe3+ measurement on account of admirable merits of rapid response, simple operation and low cost. For instance, Liu et al. [27] employed one-pot hydrothermal treatment of rose-heart radish to synthesize excitation-dependent CDs which served as an ideal probe for environmental Fe3+ determination and multicolor imaging. Niu et al. [28] constructed excitation-dependent green-fluorescent CDs-based “ON-OFF-ON-OFF” sensor for sequent specificity detection of iron ion, pyrophosphate anions and alkaline phosphatase via bottom-up electrochemical strategy by the mixtures of pyrocatechol and ethylenediamine. Lv et al. [29] proposed a novel highly crystalline excitation-dependent nitrogen-doped CDs-based sensor to track Fe3+ which were fabricated by hydrothermal carbonization method using commercial chitosan and racemic-tartaric acid. Shuang et al. [30] prepared excitation-dependent multicolor fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal pyrolysis of coffee, salvia and urea which were constructed as sensing platform to sensitively detect Fe3+ in aqueous solution and in vitro. Attractively, most of CDs-based florescent Fe3+ probes not only exhibit broadband optical absorption, but also show good chemical stability, exceptional biocompatibility and low toxicity which make them outstanding and superior to other fluorescent materials. On the other hand, these florescent probes show continuously adjustable excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) with considerable intensities, because it can present any visible color through choosing different excitation wavelengths. More importantly, the excitation-dependent PL properties will enable the CDs to integrate with a broad series of photoluminescent objects and ingeniously set up multifunctional sensing systems without considering the match of energy gaps, which facilitate them promising application for multicolor sensing and bioimaging in vivo and in vitro. However, many CDs-based fluorescent Fe3+ probes have relatively higher detection limits, which have tremendously hindered their further utilizations for the practical analysis of real samples. Hence, it is valuable to further expand these kinds of ultrasensitive and effective CDs-based sensors with excellent optical properties along with lower detection limit.

In this work, blue-fluorescent CDs was fabricated via hydrothermal route which employed Caulis polygoni multiflora as sole raw material without any dopants for the first time. As-acquired CDs were better proven to be ultra-low toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility along with fabulous stability. Significantly, it was found that CDs demonstrate excitation-dependent PL properties with a QY of up to 42%. Besides, as-synthesized CDs have been utilized for quantitative detection of Fe3+ levels owing to static quenching mechanism. Moreover, as-acquired CDs provide a visual sensing platform for Fe3+ determination in HepG-2 cells. Furthermore, their application in multicolor bioimaging was also investigated through zebrafish larvae with satisfying results. Inspired by these prominent features, CDs were eventually extended for tracking Fe3+ fluctuations in vivo.

Specifically, 0.5 g solid powder were dispersed in 25 mL ultrapure water which was sealed in 50 mL hydrothermal reactor vessel followed by keeping at 220 °C for 3 h. To remove large or unreacted particles, obtained crude products were first centrifuged in a high speed centrifuge under 3000 rpm for 20 min after natural cooling. Afterwards, the filtrate was further transferred to dialysis membrane for 24 h after filtering through a microporous membrane (0.22 mm). Ultimately, orange purified solution was lyophilized to acquire solid-state CDs.

Fluorescence titration experiment of CDs with Fe3+ was implemented in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.3). In a typical procedure, 80 µL CDs aqueous solution was dispersed in 300 µL Tris-HCl buffer solution. Subsequently, different contents of Fe3+ were introduced into CDs system to incubate for 30 s. After this, the PL excitation and emission of samples were recorded upon 380 nm excitation. Further, the influences of competitive substances towards CDs were evaluated instead of Fe3+ under the identical detection method.

From Fig. 1 , CDs were acquired facilely through hydrothermal pyrolysis of Caulis polygoni multiflora. As far as we know, Caulis polygoni multiflora was utilized as sole raw material for the first time which has ingredients of flavonoids and anthraquinones compounds including β-sitosterol, glycosides, tannins and chrysophanol. Apart from carbon, these compounds contain O and H elements which facilitate the preparation of CDs with functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl on their surface and thus improve water-solubility and fluorescence of CDs. In contrast to other most materials in Table S1, Caulis polygoni multiflora is not only abundant and renewable but also readily available, low cost and nontoxic. It is also notable that this starting material contributes to carbon source and the functional doping of nitrogen for the synthesis of CDs and no more additional reagents or any dopants were required.

TEM and AFM depicted the morphology and structure of CDs. Results indicated in Fig. 2 A that CDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed with a mean particle diameter of 2.3 ± 0.5 nm. Also, AFM image (Fig. 2B) strong confirmed that CDs hold spherical morphology with an average particle height of about 2.6 nm, which are in good accordance with the TEM analysis.

Further, XPS has been further comprehended insight into multiple functional groups of CDs. The XPS full spectra of obtained CDs verifies three typical peaks at 282.03 eV, 397.45 eV and 529.21 eV in Fig. 3 A, further confirming the presence of C1s, N1s and O1s. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) at binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.2 eV can be readily recognized, corresponding to C = C, C–N, C–O and C = O. It clearly presents that two peaks (Fig. 3C) at 400.0 eV and 402.0 eV were assigned to C–N–C and N–H, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) can be fitted into two components at 532.1 eV and 533.4 eV, arising from C = O and C–O–C bonds, respectively. At the same time, FTIR was implemented to survey the composition and surface states of CDs in Fig. 3E. Results showed that the surface of as-acquired CDs consist of C − H (2932 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1), C = O (1775 cm−1), COO− (1584 cm−1), benzene ring (1514 cm−1), C–O (1271 cm−1 and 1194 cm−1) and C–N (1073 cm−1). All the above results revealed that the surface of as-synthesized CDs contain many carboxy and amino groups. Besides, different kinds of functional groups (C = O, C–O–C, C–H) are presented on the surface of CDs, forming “surface states” energy levels, which may lead to a series of emissive traps. Notably, the competition among different emission centers and traps dominates the optical properties of CDs [31–36].

Next, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were examined to describe the optical features of CDs. It was observed that a strong absorption peak at 278 nm corresponds to the π–π* transitions of C = C bond, while the absorption band at 370 nm is due to the n–π* transitions of the C = O [6,37]. As is known to all, most of published reports have strongly proved that the origin of blue-coded emission is quite stable in terms of energy and most possibly arises from absorption into the n-π* band. Therefore, the excitation is consistent with the n-π* band rather than the π–π* transitions [38–39]. Upon optimal excitation at 380 nm, the maximum emission peak of CDs is located at 461 nm. From Fig. 4 B, an evident excitation-dependent characteristic was showed as the excitation wavelengths altering from 340 nm to 430 nm, which were probably associated with a size-dependent luminescence or the existence of different emissive traps on CDs [10]. That is, when CDs are illuminated upon different excitation wavelengths, CDs emit different emission. As described in Fig. 4C, fluorescence emissions of CDs were calculated using CIE coordinates (0.14, 0.06). Significantly, fluorescence QY of CDs can reach 42% by comparing with quinine sulfate, which is higher than that of the synthesis of CDs from different materials to the best of knowledge.

Meanwhile, the stability of CDs solution was examined to facilitate their practical applications. Initially, the fluorescence changes of CDs in physiological pH environment were presented in Fig. S1A. It can be distinctly seen that PL intensity has a slight fluctuation in physiological pH environment, but is basically stable on broad pH values at range of 7–9. Moreover, Fig. S1B exhibited that the PL intensity of CDs has no obvious effect at various concentration levels of NaCl (0 M−1 M). Finally, PL intensity of CDs keeps steady even at UV excitation for 2 h in Fig. S1C. Besides, the aqueous solution of CDs still displays as transparent orange solution under daylight without obvious precipitation and the fluorescence intensity of CDs also exhibits almost invariable as shown in Fig. S1D after 2 months of storage in a refrigerator. In other words, above evidences fiercely expressed that CDs exhibit superb optical stability and provide potential utilization for biological sample analysis and bioimaging investigations in on-site and long duration.

Taking advantage of these fabulous features of CDs, we further probed their potential sensing applications. Fig. 5 A presented that relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) was dramatically reduced only in the existence of Fe3+, while there were nearly no signal effects over other competitive substances, which undoubtedly certificated that as-prepared CDs can selectively monitor Fe3+ without remarkable interference and serve as a fluorescent sensor to directly identify Fe3+. Moreover, these results illustrated in Fig. 5B that the PL intensity of CDs gradually decreased as the amounts of Fe3+ raising (up to 400 μM). Fig. 5C indicated an excellent linear relationship between Ln (F/F0 + 0.11) and Fe3+ concentrations (R2 = 0.996) in the range of 0 μM–400 μM. In particular, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.025 μM, which is lower than those previously reported. These consequences implied that as-acquired CDs can serve as an ultrasensitive indicator to specifically identify Fe3+.

The thorough explorations for interaction between Fe3+ and CDs were implemented and presented in Fig. 6 A–D. Originally, it was found in Fig. 6A that the excitation or emission band of CDs exhibited hardly no overlap with the absorption band of Fe3+, which indicated that possible fluorescence sensing mechanism does not support internal filter effect [10]. Fig. 6A showed that the absorption peak of Fe3+ at 293 nm was ascribed to the d → d transition of the ligand field perturbation of hydrated iron ions [40], which disappeared due to the formation of the complex between CDs and Fe3+. That is, as-synthesized CDs and Fe3+ completely interacted to generate a new substance and there are no free iron ions in the solution, which caused the disappearance of the iron ion peak. Further evidences in Fig. 6B demonstrated that excited-state fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs/Fe3+ are 3.3176 ns and 3.2746 ns, proving a tendency towards the static quenching mechanism [41]. Also, an apparent variation in the absorption intensities in Fig. 6C was clearly observed when the amounts of Fe3+ constantly increased (up to 400 μM). This evidence further confirmed that the quenching mechanism may be relevant to static quenching mechanism which forms a steady and non-fluorescent ground state complexation between CDs and Fe3+. For the purpose of comprehending insight into the fluorescence sensing mechanism, we explored the zeta potential of CDs and CDs/Fe3+. From Fig. 6D, the zeta potential values presented a significant change after addition of Fe3+ from − 10.85 mV to + 1.24 mV, indicating that CDs reacted with Fe3+ to generate a Fe3+-CDs complex [42].

It is well known that CDs have been frequently utilized as eminent multicolor biolabeling agents for cell imaging field. Encouraged by above the probability of CDs, we have implemented MTT assay to appraise the cytotoxicity of CDs with HepG-2 cells. As depicted in Fig. S2, it was seen that cell survival rate is more than 80%, which confirmed as-prepared CDs hold ultra-low cytotoxicity in vitro and are suitable to track Fe3+ in living cells.

Benefiting from the excitation dependency of CDs, the application of CDs in multicolor bioimaging was further carried out. Further, HepG-2 cells showed bright blue, green and orange fluorescence by excitation of 405, 488 and 514 nm and the merging images of three panels were presented in Fig. 7 . With introducing Fe3+ in the culture medium, gradual drops in the fluorescence brightness of HepG-2 cells were captured within 40 s in Fig. 8 . Satisfactory consequences illustrated that acquired CDs with favorable cell permeability were capable of tracking Fe3+ dynamics in living cells.

To validate whether zebrafish larvae labeled with different amounts of CDs were ultra-low toxicity, a series of samples with changing concentrations of CDs were executed and assessed the long-term viability of zebrafish larvae. As shown in Fig. S3, when zebrafish larvae exposed to CDs even up to 0.5 mg/mL for 3 days at 28 °C, the viability of zebrafish larvae is higher up to 90%, which indicated the superior biocompatibility and ultra-low cytotoxicity of as-prepared CDs. Fig. 9 A1 − 3 presented that zebrafish larvae stained with 0.5 mg/mL CDs exhibited distinct fluorescent signal in both blue, green and red channels, and it was observed in the entire zebrafish body including tissues, organs and even skeletal structures such as vertebrae and cleithrum. Fig. 9A5 − 7 displayed an enlarged view of images of zebrafish larvae which better proved that as-prepared CDs is tissue-permeable through blood circulation and capable of imaging in vivo. Meanwhile, confocal images of zebrafish larvae in media at 0.2 mg/mL CDs also confirmed that obtained CDs can be implemented as an effective nanosensor for in vivo bioimaging and expected to be an ideal candidate in smart drug delivery and bioimaging therapeutic agents.

Prompted by bioimaging application of CDs in vivo, we further quested the biodistribution and biocompatibility of CDs in nude mice to certificate whether obtained CDs can efficiently penetrate mouse skin and tissue. In the process of experiments, no animals indicated abnormal phenomenon of acute toxicological response, which revealed that obtained CDs hold ultralow toxicity and could be implemented as potential biolabeling agent in vivo. Biodistribution images were successfully captured at 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9 h via tail intravenous injection of CDs in nude mice. It is observed in Fig. 10 that evident fluorescence signals spread over the whole body of mouse at 1 h after the injection, showing that mouse skin and tissues can be smoothly permeated by CDs. Within the 7 h after injection of CDs, fluorescence signals still concentrated in thoracic, intestinal and bladder regions. At 9 h, the fluorescence intensity progressively weakened in the thoracic region with a spot detected in the bladder region, which implied that CDs own fabulous biocompatibility and can be rapidly excreted from the gastrointestinal system.

Moreover, after subsequent injection of Fe3+, fluorescence intensity of mice injected with CDs emitted a remarkable drop as depicted in Fig. 11 , which indicated that CDs can visualize variations of Fe3+ in nude mice. It is worth mentioning that mouse imaging experiments generally demanded that probes possess a near-infrared fluorescence emission, but our proposed CDs with short-wavelength emission have achieved in vivo imaging. More importantly, in vivo imaging experiments demonstrated that CDs can be applied as a disease detection tool in physiology and pathology.

In summary, we report a novel CDs with high QY of 42% using Caulis polygoni multiflora as sole precursor via hydrothermal route. As-acquired CDs represent features of excitation dependency, outstanding biocompatibility along with admirable stability. Owing to static quenching effect, obtained CDs have been applied to sensitively monitor Fe3+ both in aqueous solution and in living cells. Intriguingly, multicolor bioimaging of zebrafish larvae was also achieved. Besides, acquired CDs were successfully utilized for the real-time detection of Fe3+ in vivo with satisfying results. This work not only contributes to the use of renewable natural material to produce CDs with high QY, but also develops a fast and convenient analytical platform to track Fe3+ both in vitro and in vivo. Particularly, this work proved that CDs-based probe is a powerful tool in future biological and medical areas.

Dan Chang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhonghua Zhao: Investigation, Validation. Wenjing Niu: Investigation, Data curation. Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Yongxing Yang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talent Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119964.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of N, Zn-doped carbon dots for the detection of Fe3+ ions and bactericidal activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus
J. Photochem. Photobiol., B

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly luminescent N-doped carbon dots as a fluorescence detecting platform for Fe3+ in solutions and living cells

Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

Analyst
